# Last Time

::: {.incremental}
+ **Modular Programming**
+ **Coding Functions**
+ **Passing Data to a Function**
+ **Returning Data from a Function**
+ **Void Functions**
:::

::: notes
Brief recall of lecture 04.01. Students should remember that functions give names to pieces of processing, parameters move data into a function, and return values send one answer back.
:::

<!-- Slide 1 -->

# Today's Agenda

- Function Contracts
- Function Boundary Model
- Passing Vectors
- Scope
- Shadow Variables
- Stack Frame Model

::: notes

:::

<!-- Slide 2 -->

{{< include topic_1_function_contracts.qmd >}}
{{< include topic_2_function_boundary.qmd >}}
{{< include topic_3_passing_vectors.qmd >}}
{{< include topic_4_scope.qmd >}}
{{< include topic_5_shadow_variables.qmd >}}
{{< include topic_6_stack_frame.qmd >}}

---

# Key Takeaways

- A function contract tells the caller what a function needs and what it promises to do.
- A function boundary keeps caller responsibilities and function responsibilities separate.
- Vectors can cross function boundaries through parameters and return values.
- Scope controls where a name is visible.
- Shadow variables hide another visible name and can make a trace misleading.
- Stack frames explain why function variables are temporary, local, and separate.

<!-- Slide 73 -->

---

# Next Time

- Function practice with references
- Function overloading
- Static local variables

<!-- Slide 74 -->

---

# Appendix

<!-- Slide 75 -->

---

## Function Contracts: Good Practices

- Write the prototype before writing the function body.
- Use a name that describes the promised action or result.
- Keep the parameter list limited to data the function actually needs.

<!-- Slide 76 -->

---

## Function Contracts: Common Mistakes

- Naming a function after implementation details instead of purpose.
- Giving a function hidden dependencies that do not appear in the prototype.
- Mixing unrelated jobs into one function.

<!-- Slide 77 -->

---

## Function Contracts: More Examples

```cpp
double average(vector<int> scores);
void displayScores(vector<int> scores);
vector<int> getScores();
```

Each prototype gives the caller a different promise.

<!-- Slide 78 -->

---

## Function Contracts: Practice Problems

1. Write a prototype for a function that displays a course title.
2. Write a prototype for a function that calculates the larger of two numbers.
3. Identify the caller responsibility and function responsibility in `double average(vector<int> scores);`.

<!-- Slide 79 -->

---

## Function Contracts: AI Search Terms

- "C++ function contract beginner"
- "C++ function prototype parameters return value"
- "C++ modular function design"

<!-- Slide 80 -->

---

## Function Contracts: Questions for Reflection

- Why can a prototype tell you a lot before you read the function body?
- What makes a function promise unclear?

<!-- Slide 81 -->

---

## Function Boundary Model: Good Practices

- Pass data into a function through parameters.
- Send one answer back with a return value when the caller needs a result.
- Keep temporary details inside the function body.

<!-- Slide 82 -->

---

## Function Boundary Model: Common Mistakes

- Expecting a local variable inside a function to be visible in `main()`.
- Passing more data than the function needs.
- Hiding important data flow through global variables.

<!-- Slide 83 -->

---

## Function Boundary Model: More Examples

```cpp
int total(vector<int> scores);
void displayTotal(int total);
bool isPassing(int score);
```

The boundary is visible in each parameter list and return type.

<!-- Slide 84 -->

---

## Function Boundary Model: Practice Problems

1. Draw the boundary around a function definition.
2. Mark which values cross into the function.
3. Mark which value crosses back to the caller.

<!-- Slide 85 -->

---

## Function Boundary Model: AI Search Terms

- "C++ function parameter boundary"
- "C++ local variable function scope"
- "C++ function return value caller"

<!-- Slide 86 -->

---

## Function Boundary Model: Questions for Reflection

- Why is hidden data flow hard to debug?
- How does a clear boundary make a function easier to trust?

<!-- Slide 87 -->

---

## Passing Vectors: Good Practices

- Pass a vector when the function needs the collection as a whole.
- Pass one element at a time when each call only needs one value.
- Return a vector when the function creates a collection for the caller.

<!-- Slide 88 -->

---

## Passing Vectors: Common Mistakes

- Forgetting `#include <vector>`.
- Passing a whole vector when the function only needs one value.
- Expecting a pass-by-value parameter to change the caller's vector.

<!-- Slide 89 -->

---

## Passing Vectors: More Examples

```cpp
int countPassing(vector<int> scores);
void displayName(string name);
vector<string> getNames();
```

The parameter type should match the data each function needs.

<!-- Slide 90 -->

---

## Passing Vectors: Practice Problems

1. Decide whether `displayScore` should receive one `int` or a `vector<int>`.
2. Write a prototype for a function that returns a vector of prices.
3. Trace whether a vector parameter is the same variable as the caller's vector.

<!-- Slide 91 -->

---

## Passing Vectors: AI Search Terms

- "C++ pass vector to function"
- "C++ return vector from function"
- "C++ range based for pass element to function"

<!-- Slide 92 -->

---

## Passing Vectors: Questions for Reflection

- When is passing a whole vector clearer than passing one element?
- Why does the function contract matter more when the data is a collection?

<!-- Slide 93 -->

---

## Scope: Good Practices

- Declare variables close to where they are used.
- Pass data through parameters instead of depending on global variables.
- Keep temporary variables inside the function that needs them.

<!-- Slide 94 -->

---

## Scope: Common Mistakes

- Trying to use a variable after its block has ended.
- Assuming a parameter name is visible in the caller.
- Making a variable global only because a function needs it.

<!-- Slide 95 -->

---

## Scope: More Examples

```cpp
void displayDouble(int number) {
    int doubled = number * 2;
    cout << doubled << endl;
}
```

`number` and `doubled` belong to `displayDouble()`.

<!-- Slide 96 -->

---

## Scope: Practice Problems

1. Mark where a loop variable is visible.
2. Mark where a function parameter is visible.
3. Rewrite a global variable example using a parameter.

<!-- Slide 97 -->

---

## Scope: AI Search Terms

- "C++ function scope local variables"
- "C++ parameters local variables"
- "C++ global variables beginner"

<!-- Slide 98 -->

---

## Scope: Questions for Reflection

- Why does modular design depend on local names?
- When does making a variable visible in more places make the program harder to understand?

<!-- Slide 99 -->

---

## Shadow Variables: Good Practices

- Use different names when two variables mean different things.
- Watch for declarations inside nested blocks.
- Prefer names that describe the role of the value.

<!-- Slide 100 -->

---

## Shadow Variables: Common Mistakes

- Declaring a new variable when you meant to update an existing one.
- Reusing a parameter name inside the same function.
- Assuming the outer variable changed because the visible name changed.

<!-- Slide 101 -->

---

## Shadow Variables: More Examples

```cpp
int count = 0;

for (int count : scores) {
    cout << count << endl;
}
```

The loop variable hides the outer `count` inside the loop.

<!-- Slide 102 -->

---

## Shadow Variables: Practice Problems

1. Circle every declaration in a short program.
2. Draw boxes around each scope.
3. Identify which declaration each variable use refers to.

<!-- Slide 103 -->

---

## Shadow Variables: AI Search Terms

- "C++ shadow variable"
- "C++ name hiding local variable"
- "C++ -Wshadow warning"

<!-- Slide 104 -->

---

## Shadow Variables: Questions for Reflection

- Why can a shadow variable compile but still be wrong?
- Why does the closest visible declaration win?

<!-- Slide 105 -->

---

## Stack Frame Model: Good Practices

- Trace one active function call at a time.
- Put parameters and local variables in the frame where they belong.
- Remove a frame when its function returns.

<!-- Slide 106 -->

---

## Stack Frame Model: Common Mistakes

- Keeping a local variable alive after the function returns.
- Treating a parameter as the caller's variable.
- Forgetting that each function call gets its own frame.

<!-- Slide 107 -->

---

## Stack Frame Model: More Examples

```cpp
int square(int number) {
    int result = number * number;
    return result;
}
```

`number` and `result` live in the `square()` frame.

<!-- Slide 108 -->

---

## Stack Frame Model: Practice Problems

1. Draw the stack before a function call.
2. Add a frame for the called function.
3. Remove the frame and write the returned value in the caller.

<!-- Slide 109 -->

---

## Stack Frame Model: AI Search Terms

- "C++ stack frame beginner"
- "C++ call stack function parameters"
- "C++ local variables stack frame"

<!-- Slide 110 -->

---

## Stack Frame Model: Questions for Reflection

- How do stack frames explain why local variables disappear?
- How does the stack frame model make scope easier to trace?

<!-- Slide 111 -->
